ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for kolo mee specifically; closest parent 'noodle dish' (FOODON_00002473) used as anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (Pasta and noodles, cooked) is the closest defensible parent; no specific kolo mee or dry tossed noodle code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept maps to kolo mee or this specific vegan noodle preparation.

main_ingredients: yuba (fried tofu skin / bean curd skin)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of dry kolomee with minced soy and fried yuba, approximately 350 g, as typically served at a Singapore café or casual dining restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Kolo Mee (dry, cooked) — composite estimate from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Noodles, egg, cooked, enriched (NDB 20110); Soy protein concentrate (NDB 16122); Tofu, fried (NDB 16100); and FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table for Southeast Asia (2nd ed.) noodle dish entries
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for 'Dry Kolomee with Minced Soy and Fried Yuba' exists in any approved primary source. Values are estimated by weighted-ingredient composition: approximately 60% cooked egg noodles, 20% minced soy protein, 15% fried yuba, 5% sauce/oil. USDA SR Legacy entries used for each component. Sodium estimated from typical soy-sauce-based seasoning in similar dishes. Values represent a plausible per-100g composite for this vegan noodle dish.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of egg noodles and mixed noodle dishes — analogous estimate based on 'Noodles, instant, cooked' (GI ~47) and 'Pasta, cooked' (GI ~46–49) from the University of Sydney GI Database; adjusted for protein and fat content of soy and yuba components which lower postprandial glucose response
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for dry kolomee with minced soy and fried yuba. GI of 47 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entries for cooked egg noodles and pasta (GI range 46–52, low category). The substantial protein content from soy mince (~9.8 g/100g) and fat from fried yuba further attenuate glycemic response, supporting a low GI estimate. GL per serving = (47 × 26.5 × 3.5) / 100 = 43.5; corrected for 350g serving: (47 × 92.75) / 100 ≈ 43.6. GL reported as 61.6 using (GI × carb_per_serving_g) / 100 = (47 × 92.75) / 100 = 43.6. Recalculated: carbohydrate per serving = 26.5 g/100g × 350g / 100 = 92.75g; GL = (47 × 92.75) / 100 = 43.6. Value corrected to 43.6.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 420 mg per 100g; a 350g serving delivers approximately 1470 mg sodium, representing 64% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2300 mg
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to half a bowl and request reduced soy sauce seasoning, as the sodium load from soy-based sauces in this dish is clinically significant.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~47) due to egg noodle base and high protein from soy mince, but total carbohydrate per serving (~93g) produces a high glycemic load of approximately 43.6
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes may benefit from the low GI of this dish, but should be aware that the large carbohydrate load per standard serving can significantly raise postprandial blood glucose; a half-portion with additional non-starchy vegetables is advisable.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat is relatively low (~1.1 g/100g) and cholesterol is zero (vegan), but sodium content is high and the fried yuba component contributes oxidised polyunsaturated fats
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The vegan profile and absence of dietary cholesterol are favourable for cardiovascular health, but the high sodium content and deep-fried yuba component warrant moderation; individuals with cardiovascular disease should request steamed or unfried yuba where possible.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dry Kolomee with Minced Soy and Fried Yuba is a fully vegan adaptation of kolo mee, a Sarawakian-origin dry tossed noodle dish popular in Singapore. The dish consists of springy wheat egg-style noodles (or vegan egg-free noodles) tossed in a savoury sauce of soy sauce, sesame oil, and a small amount of vegetable oil, topped with seasoned minced textured soy protein (a plant-based substitute for minced pork) and crispy fried yuba (tofu skin or bean curd skin). Yuba is produced by lifting the skin that forms on heated soy milk; when deep-fried it becomes golden, crispy, and rich in plant protein and fat. The dish is served dry — meaning the noodles are drained and tossed rather than served in broth — giving a concentrated, umami-rich flavour profile with a chewy noodle texture contrasted by the crispy yuba. Nutritionally, the dish provides approximately 198 kcal per 100g, with a meaningful protein contribution (~9.8 g/100g) from both the soy mince and yuba, making it more satiating than plain noodle dishes. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 47 (low), supported by the protein and fat content that attenuates glucose absorption, though the large carbohydrate load per standard serving (~93g per bowl) results in a high glycemic load. Sodium is a primary clinical concern at approximately 420 mg/100g due to soy sauce seasoning. The dish is free from animal products, dairy, and cholesterol, making it suitable for vegan and lactose-intolerant individuals, though it contains gluten (wheat noodles) and soy allergens. It is served at Flavours by Saute at Funan Mall, Singapore, and represents the growing trend of plant-based adaptations of traditional Southeast Asian noodle dishes.
common_user_queries: Calories in dry kolo mee Singapore
What is minced soy in noodles?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for this specific vegan kolomee dish exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated by weighted-ingredient composition using USDA SR Legacy component entries. GI is estimated by analogy from University of Sydney GI Database entries for cooked egg noodles and pasta. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level sources are high quality and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates; no single approved-source entry exists for this exact dish. Values should be reviewed if HPB Singapore Foodo adds a kolomee entry. 2. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reliably reported across the component USDA SR Legacy entries used and cannot be defensibly estimated for this composite. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived vitamin D sources; not reported in component sources. 4. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not consistently reported across the component USDA SR Legacy entries used. 5. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the component USDA SR Legacy entries used for this composite estimate. 6. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this is a fully vegan dish containing no animal products; confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy entries for all components (soy protein, tofu skin, plant oils). 7. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a fully plant-based dish with no fortified ingredients reported; individuals relying on this dish should supplement B12. 8. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy component entries; no partially hydrogenated oils used. 9. GI of 47 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database (cooked egg noodles/pasta range 46–52); no direct measurement exists for this dish. 10. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (47 × 92.75g carbohydrate per 350g serving) / 100 = 43.6. 11. Halal status is marked false as the restaurant (Flavours by Saute) is not listed as a halal-certified establishment; this should be verified independently. 12. The dish is tagged under 'Global' cuisine in the source data; region of origin is noted as Singapore (Sarawak-inspired) reflecting the kolo mee heritage.

improvement_actions_taken: Constructed full multilingual local_names for Singaporean dish requirement (Chinese simplified/traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi).
Built composite nutrient estimate from USDA SR Legacy component entries with transparent methodology.
Estimated GI of 47 from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries (cooked egg noodles/pasta) with full documentation.
Calculated glycemic load per serving as (47 × 92.75) / 100 = 43.6 for 350g serving.
Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, selenium_mcg) with explicit reasons in notes.
Documented all 0.0 values (cholesterol_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, trans_fat_g) with zero-justification phrases in notes.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases consistency — all language-specific strings appear verbatim in flat aliases.
Set ontology match_type values to only allowed values: 'closest_parent' and 'none'.
Set gi_category to 'low' consistent with GI 47 ≤ 55 threshold.
Wrote health context for all three required conditions with specific clinical guidance.
